title: for loop — counted repetition
successMessage: Three parts: init (int i = 1), condition (i <= 5), update (i++). Java runs the init once, then loops while condition is true, running the update after each iteration.

title: for-each — iterate over arrays
successMessage: for-each is cleaner and safer — no off-by-one index mistakes. Use it whenever you need all elements. Use classic for when you need the index or want to step by 2+.

title: while — loop until condition changes
instruction:
`while` loops while the condition is true. Use it when you don't know upfront how many iterations you need.

⚠️ Always ensure the condition will eventually become false — infinite loops hang your program.

Simulate a number guessing game.
successMessage: while checked the condition BEFORE each iteration. The loop stopped as soon as guess == secret. while is perfect for 'keep going until something happens'.

title: do-while — run at least once
instruction:
`do-while` runs the body FIRST, then checks the condition. Guaranteed to run at least once.

Perfect for: menus (show then validate), input validation (prompt then check).
successMessage: The body ran before the condition was checked. 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 → 16 → 32 — stopped when 32 >= 20. do-while is rarer than while but essential for 'run once then validate' patterns.

title: break — exit early
instruction:
`break` immediately exits the loop. Once you find what you're looking for, stop — no point continuing.

Search for the first prime number above 10.
successMessage: Found 11 (prime), printed it, and broke out. Numbers 12-100 were never checked. In a database with millions of records, finding row 3 and breaking is essential.

title: continue — skip this iteration
instruction:
`continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and goes to the next one.

Print only odd numbers 1-10 using continue.

codeChecks.message: Use continue to skip even numbers.

successMessage: Even numbers skipped, odd numbers printed. break exits the loop, continue skips the iteration. Two different tools — know which one you need.

title: Nested loops
instruction:
Loops inside loops. Inner loop runs completely for every outer iteration.

Print a 4×4 multiplication table.

codeChecks.message: Use nested loops — a loop inside a loop.

successMessage: printf with %3d right-aligns in 3 spaces — that's how the table lines up. Nested loops power tables, matrices, and pair comparisons everywhere.

title: FizzBuzz — the classic interview test
instruction:
Almost every programming interview includes this problem.

- Print 1 to 30
- Multiple of 3: print `Fizz`
- Multiple of 5: print `Buzz`
- Multiple of both: print `FizzBuzz`

Check 15 (both) first, or it can never trigger.
hint: Check % 15 first, then % 3, then % 5, then print the number.
successMessage: FizzBuzz solved! The % 15 check must come before % 3 and % 5 — otherwise 15 would match Fizz first. Ordering conditions correctly is a fundamental programming skill. Chapter 6 done!
